stories.title: Solar Oven Science Project
stories.keySummary: I made a simple solar oven that uses sunlight to warm small snacks. The project helped me understand how heat, light, and reflective surfaces work together.
stories.idea: I wanted to build something useful with clean energy. I liked the idea of cooking with sunlight because it felt fun, smart, and good for the planet.
stories.whatIBuilt: I used a cardboard box, foil, black paper, and clear plastic to trap heat inside the box. I tested different angles in the sun and saw which setup made the oven warmer and faster.
stories.whatILearned: I learned that small changes in angle and insulation can make a big difference. I also learned that science feels more exciting when I can test an idea in real life.
stories.teacherNote: Aarav showed great curiosity and patience. He tested his idea carefully and explained his results in a very clear and confident way.

stories.title: Smart Plant Watering System
stories.keySummary: I built a smart watering system that checks soil moisture and waters the plant only when it needs help. It is a simple project, but it solves a real problem at home.
stories.idea: I noticed that our plants were sometimes watered too early and sometimes too late. I wanted to make a small helper that could make a better choice than guessing every day.
stories.whatIBuilt: I used a moisture sensor, an Arduino board, wires, and a small pump idea to create a basic smart plant setup. When the soil becomes dry, the system can trigger watering and make plant care easier.
stories.whatILearned: I learned how sensors collect real-world data and how code can turn that data into an action. I also learned that testing again and again is a big part of building something useful.
stories.teacherNote: Sarah showed excellent problem-solving skills and strong ownership of her work. She improved the idea step by step and kept the project practical and easy to understand.

stories.title: 3D Printed Pencil Holder
stories.keySummary: I designed and printed a pencil holder for my study desk. It looks simple, but it helped me learn how to turn an idea into a real object.
stories.idea: My pens and pencils were always messy. I wanted to make a holder that looked neat and also had enough space for the things I use every day.
stories.whatIBuilt: I sketched a small desk organizer, created the shape in 3D software, and printed a first version. Then I changed the size and thickness so it would be stronger and more useful.
stories.whatILearned: I learned that even small products need planning, testing, and changes. I also learned how fun it feels to hold something in my hand that started as a digital design.
stories.teacherNote: Riya worked with great focus and creativity. She accepted feedback well and used it to improve both the design and the final print.

stories.title: Clean Water Awareness Poster
stories.keySummary: I made a bright poster about saving water and keeping it clean. My goal was to share one simple message in a way that younger students could understand quickly.
stories.idea: We talk a lot about water in school, but many people still waste it without thinking. I wanted to make a poster that felt clear, friendly, and easy to remember.
stories.whatIBuilt: I chose simple words, strong colours, and easy icons to explain why water matters. I also arranged the text in a way that feels easy to read from top to bottom.
stories.whatILearned: I learned that good design is not only about making something look nice. It is also about helping people understand an important message quickly.
stories.teacherNote: Ibrahim communicated a meaningful idea with strong visual choices. His work showed empathy for the audience and a clear sense of purpose.

stories.title: Clean Water Filteration
stories.keySummary: I created a simple water filter model to show how dirty water can become clearer through layers. The project helped me understand purification in a hands-on way.
stories.idea: I wanted to learn how filtration works in real life. Instead of only reading about it, I thought it would be more fun to build a small model and test it myself.
stories.whatIBuilt: I used sand, gravel, cotton, and a bottle to create a layer-based filter model. Then I poured cloudy water through it and compared the water before and after the test.
stories.whatILearned: I learned that problem solving becomes easier when I break a big system into small parts. I also learned how science experiments can help me understand important community issues.
stories.teacherNote: Meera connected classroom learning with a real-world topic in a thoughtful way. Her explanation was calm, clear, and well supported by the experiment.

stories.title: Mini Weather Tracker
stories.keySummary: I built a simple weather tracker that records temperature and basic daily changes. It helped me see how data can tell a story over time.
stories.idea: I like checking the weather every morning. I wanted to make a small project that could collect weather details and show patterns in an easy way.
stories.whatIBuilt: I made a simple tracker that stores readings and turns them into a small chart. I tested it over a few days and compared cool mornings, warm afternoons, and changes in the sky.
stories.whatILearned: I learned that data becomes much more interesting when it is organised clearly. I also learned that patterns are easier to notice when I collect information every day.
stories.teacherNote: Daniel stayed consistent and careful throughout the project. He showed strong attention to detail and used data in a clear and meaningful way.

stories.title: Motion Sensor Night Light
stories.keySummary: I made a motion sensor night light that turns on when someone walks nearby. It is a small project, but it feels helpful and real.
stories.idea: At night, it can be hard to find the switch in a dark room. I wanted to build something that reacts automatically and makes movement safer and easier.
stories.whatIBuilt: I connected a motion sensor to a light output and tested the timing. Then I adjusted the code so the light turns on smoothly and stays on just long enough to be useful.
stories.whatILearned: I learned how sensors and code can work together to solve everyday problems. I also learned that timing matters a lot when a project needs to feel natural to use.
stories.teacherNote: Zoya focused on both function and user experience. She thought carefully about how the project would feel for a real person using it at home.

stories.title: Recycled Robot Arm
stories.keySummary: I built a model robot arm using recycled materials and simple joints. The project showed me how movement and structure work together.
stories.idea: I enjoy making things with my hands, and I wanted to turn waste materials into something mechanical and interesting. A robot arm felt like the perfect challenge.
stories.whatIBuilt: I used cardboard, sticks, string, and simple connectors to build a moving arm. I tested how far it could reach and changed the joint positions to make the movement smoother.
stories.whatILearned: I learned that strong ideas do not always need expensive materials. I also learned how design, balance, and patience can turn simple parts into a working model.
stories.teacherNote: Kabir showed imagination and persistence throughout the project. He used everyday materials in a creative way and kept improving the design after each test.
